summary: I was born and raised in Kansas City, Kansas, starting school there around 1930 when I was five years old. My early memories are tied to this city, where my mother took me to kindergarten and later to Dunbar Elementary School. It was in Kansas City, Kansas that I first got involved with music and started learning instruments, surrounded by a large family and the challenges of the time.

At about ten years old, I had the chance to study music at Dunbar Elementary School in Kansas City, Kansas. It was there that I first seriously took up an instrument, sneaking out my father's alto saxophone to practice. The school offered music lessons that were quite affordable, though the teacher had to manage many students at once, which made the lessons brief but valuable.

The Works Progress Administration, or WPA, played a big role in my musical development when I was a kid in Kansas City. Around the early 1930s, I would hang around a WPA band that rehearsed in a small church on Fifth Street, and eventually they let me join in. This band was a mix of adults and older musicians, and it was through the WPA that I got to learn and play a wide variety of music.

Fifth Street in Kansas City, Kansas was the place where the WPA band rehearsed in a little church house, and I would often sneak in to listen and eventually play with them. This was a key spot for me around the early 1930s, as it was where I got to meet musicians like Virgil Hill and learn a lot about band music. It was a formative place for my musical journey as a young boy.

Virgil Hill was a musician I met in the WPA band on Fifth Street in Kansas City. He was a bit older and made a living playing in nightclubs, but he was part of the band where I first got to sit and learn by watching him. He was kind enough to let me sit with him and encouraged me to start playing when I felt ready, which really helped me grow as a young musician.

In the WPA band, there were a few French horns, which they called peck horns or mellophones back then. I remember seeing these instruments and the variety of music we played, including concert pieces and operas. Though I started on saxophone and clarinet, the presence of the French horn in the band added to the rich musical environment I was part of.

The WPA band I played with performed a wide range of music, including pieces by Puccini. I remember playing some of his operatic works during rehearsals in Kansas City, which was quite an experience for a young musician like me. It showed me the diversity of music beyond just marches and popular tunes.

One of the operas we played in the WPA band was Carmen, which left a strong impression on me. Playing that music in Kansas City during the early 1930s was part of my early exposure to classical and operatic works. It was exciting to be part of such a varied musical program as a young player.

Semper Fidelis was one of the marches we regularly played in the WPA band in Kansas City. It’s a classic John Philip Sousa march, and performing it was part of my early band experience in the 1930s. Playing marches like this helped me develop my skills and appreciation for band music.

John Philip Sousa’s marches were a staple in the repertoire of the WPA band I played with in Kansas City during the 1930s. Pieces like Semper Fidelis and The Washington Post were among the favorites we performed. Sousa’s music was a big part of my early musical education and enjoyment.

The Washington Post march was one of the John Philip Sousa pieces we played with the WPA band in Kansas City. I remember learning and performing it along with other marches and popular tunes during those early years in the 1930s. It was part of the diverse music that shaped my band experience.

Bourbon Street Parade was another lively tune we played in the WPA band in Kansas City during the early 1930s. It added a fun, upbeat element to our concerts alongside marches and operatic pieces. Playing such varied music kept me engaged and excited about being in the band.

Louis Whitworth was a clarinet player in the WPA band and a big influence on me when I was about twelve. He had studied with a symphony player in Kansas City, Missouri, and was considered the best clarinetist around. When I showed interest in the clarinet, Louis and the band members chipped in to buy me one, which was a huge encouragement for my musical growth.

Though I grew up in Kansas City, Kansas, I had connections to Kansas City, Missouri, especially through musicians like Louis Whitworth. He studied with a symphony player there, which showed me the level of musical training available in the area. The two cities were closely linked in my musical life during the 1930s.
